quote: I didn't fill out any solar form, but I wouldn't be surprised if my wife did.
stewart_read: Andy's opening line tells Bucky two things simultaneously: (1) the wife is the initiating decision-maker, and (2) Andy is not personally invested in the call yet. Bucky acknowledged and moved on without asking whether the wife should be part of the appointment. This is the first of two missed spouse-inclusion opportunities.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: Bucky may have intended to circle back to spouse inclusion at button-up. Evidence does not support this — he never returned to it.
coaching_implication: Manager should replay 00:36 and ask Bucky: 'What did you do with that line?' The correct move per protocols.spouse_decision is to immediately ask whether the wife would be available for the appointment. Bucky had the information and didn't use it. This is the root cause of the fragile booking.

stewart_read: Second touch of the spouse pattern. Bucky confirms the wife is on title and moves directly to utility company. At this point the protocol is unambiguous: both title-holders should be confirmed on the appointment. Bucky did not ask. The appointment was booked with Andy alone — the person who did NOT fill out the form.
coaching_implication: This is the cleaner coaching moment of the two spouse touches because the title confirmation is explicit. Manager should ask: 'She's on the title — what does the codex say you do next?' The answer is: ask what time works for both of them. Bucky skipped it.

quote: That's something that I don't pay any attention to. I'm a retired soldier. Oh. A physicist, electrical engineer electrical engineer and a mathematician with three PhDs. So I pay attention to math and science, not the money.
stewart_read: Andy's credential disclosure is not a deflection — it's a trust gift and a qualification signal. A retired military physicist with three PhDs who 'pays attention to math and science' is telling Bucky exactly how to frame the closer's appointment: lead with the engineering logic of the system design, not the savings pitch. Bucky responded with a filler pause and moved to the next verify item.
coaching_implication: Manager should ask: 'What did Andy just tell you about how to sell him?' The answer is: he wants the math, not the marketing. The move here is a brief acknowledgment that bridges to the closer: 'That's actually perfect — our specialist builds the design from your actual usage data, so you'll see the real numbers, not a ballpark.' Bucky had the hook and didn't use it.

quote: I'm running your text through a, an AI system to find out whether you're real or bullshit.
stewart_read: Andy is running an active legitimacy check mid-call. This is the third touch of the vetting pattern. Bucky waited passively and was cleared. The strategic read would be that Bucky had already established enough legitimacy to survive the check — but the evidence doesn't support deliberate setup. Bucky never offered Ion's Tesla installer status, website, or physical address at any point. He passed by luck of Andy's AI tool, not by proactive legitimacy anchoring.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: Bucky's calm, unhurried tone throughout the call may have been sufficient legitimacy signaling. Possible, but Bucky offered no concrete legitimacy anchors at any point — the passive read is more supported.
coaching_implication: Manager should use this moment to introduce the Tesla intro framing from intros.tesla. Had Bucky opened with 'We're the certified solar installer for Tesla in Virginia,' Andy's AI check would have had a concrete anchor to verify. The legitimacy gate would have opened faster and with less risk.

quote: It's actually worthless to give me an estimate without actually seeing the house, seeing the way it stands, what the local geometry and geography are, how it points with respect to north and south.
stewart_read: Andy is not raising an objection — he is articulating exactly why an in-person assessment is necessary. This is a high-qualification signal: he has already reasoned through the problem and concluded that a serious evaluation requires a site visit. Bucky accommodated the request but did not anchor it as the reason the appointment matters. The enthusiasm was left unmirrored.
coaching_implication: The move here is to mirror Andy's engineering logic back to him: 'Exactly — that's why our specialist comes out to do a full site assessment, not just a satellite estimate.' Bucky said 'You want somebody to come over in person?' — a flat confirmation that missed the opportunity to validate Andy's reasoning and build appointment commitment.

quote: My bills over the last year are between 155 and 196.
stewart_read: Willa delivers the bill range. Bucky says 'Okay' and immediately asks for the kilowatt usage graph. The bill number was captured and dropped — no acknowledgment, no flip, no emotional alignment against the utility. Per coaching_philosophy.bill_anchoring, capture alone is necessary but not sufficient. The flip was available and unused.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: Bucky may have been deferring the bill flip to the closer. Not supported — the bill anchor is a setter-stage move, and there is no evidence Bucky planned to return to it.
coaching_implication: Manager should replay 04:20 and ask: 'You had her monthly number — what did you do with it?' The move is to name that number as the problem the specialist is solving, not as a data point for the design. Something like: 'So you're spending up to $196 a month right now — that's exactly what our specialist is going to show you how to address.' No fabricated savings figures needed; the pain is in the number she already gave.

quote: I'll put you on the calendar. K. I'll text you after to remind you also, and then they'll call you around 11:00.
stewart_read: Bucky books the appointment but delivers none of the required button-up elements: no one-hour block statement, no not-driving/at-computer anchor, no 'is that fair' handshake. The booking is also structurally incomplete because the wife — the initiating decision-maker and title co-holder — is not confirmed as present. This is a thin booking with elevated no-show risk.
coaching_implication: Manager should walk through the button_up checklist against this moment item by item. Bucky hit one element (text follow-up promise) and missed four. The 'is that fair' handshake alone is predictive of show rate per the codex. This is a mechanical gap, not a judgment call — Bucky needs to drill the button-up sequence until it's automatic.

stewart_read: Andy mentions three adults in the household. Bucky does not follow up on who the third adult is or whether they have any role in the decision. Combined with the wife being the form-filler and a title co-holder, the household composition is more complex than a standard two-person decision. The third adult could be an adult child with financial involvement — a potential co-decision-maker per protocols.adult_child_decision_maker.
coaching_implication: Manager should flag this for the handoff brief: the closer walks into a three-adult household where the wife initiated the inquiry and Andy is the one who booked. Who is the third adult and what is their role? Bucky should have asked. Add to the handoff brief as an unresolved household composition question.
